hook: You already know how to count simple collections — but what happens when objects overlap, repeat, or follow hidden patterns that make direct counting feel impossible? Contest problems love to hide structure that turns a seemingly chaotic count into an elegant formula. This unit hands you the toolkit that competitors use to tame even the most tangled counting problems.
concept: Combinatorics is the art of counting cleverly: instead of listing everything, you find structure, symmetry, or correspondence that makes the answer fall out naturally — captured formally by the idea of constructing *bijections, recursions, and generating functions* to transform hard counts into tractable ones.

topic_roadmap.description: Master the foundational methods — multiplication, permutations, combinations, and one-to-one correspondences — so you can reframe any counting problem as one you already know how to solve.
Apply the surprisingly powerful idea that if $n+1$ objects occupy $n$ boxes, some box holds two, to crack existence and extremal problems that seem to offer no equation to work with.
Use the inclusion-exclusion principle to count unions of overlapping sets precisely by alternately adding and subtracting intersection sizes, turning messy overlaps into clean formulas.
Build recurrence relations that express a count in terms of smaller cases, then solve or verify them with combinatorial reasoning to handle problems where direct formulas are hard to see.
Encode sequences of counts as coefficients of power series like $\sum a_n x^n$ and manipulate these polynomials algebraically to extract answers to advanced counting and partition problems.

Prove and exploit identities such as Vandermonde's or the Hockey Stick by counting the same quantity two different ways, a technique that unlocks elegant solutions on the hardest competition problems.
